scenario: A Service Task process-data is inside an Embedded Subprocess. The subprocess scope sets a local variable tempCounter. The team wants to know: is tempCounter accessible outside the subprocess after it completes?
question: Which is true about variable scope?

options.text: All variables in Camunda 8 are global process-level variables; an Embedded Subprocess scope does not create any isolation. This is factually incorrect. Variables created inside a subprocess via local scoping mechanisms are visible only within that subprocess scope and are not automatically propagated to the parent process scope without explicit Output Mappings.

Variables set inside an Embedded Subprocess are deleted permanently when the subprocess completes. Variables scoped to the subprocess via Input Mappings were never promoted to the parent scope, so they are not explicitly deleted -- they simply were never visible in the parent scope to begin with. Using Output Mapping on the subprocess can propagate specific values upward before completion.

Variables set anywhere in a process instance persist forever and are visible in every scope throughout the instance lifetime. This is factually incorrect. Camunda 8 scoping mechanisms via Input and Output Mappings limit which variables are visible within or outside a subprocess. Variables configured with subprocess-level local scoping are isolated from the parent. Process-level variables created without subprocess scoping remain visible across the whole instance.

Variables set inside an Embedded Subprocess via local scoping through Input/Output Mappings are scoped to the subprocess and not visible outside unless propagated via output mapping on the subprocess element. Process-level variables set by elements outside the subprocess scope remain visible throughout. This scoping model lets modellers control exactly which data crosses subprocess boundaries in either direction.

a.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 variables are scoped. A variable created within a subprocess scope (e.g., via that subprocess's Input/Output Mapping or by a task within it that scopes a local var) is visible only inside that scope. Use Output Mapping on the subprocess to propagate to parent scope.

c.text: Incorrect. Scoped variables go out of scope (effectively cleaned up) when scope ends, but it's not "deletion" semantics -- they were never in the parent scope.
